From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Name that a business trades under for commercial purposes

This article is about businesses. For cultivated land plants, see [Cultivar § Trade designations and selling names](/wiki/Cultivar#Trade_designations_and_selling_names "Cultivar"). For pharmaceuticals, see [Drug nomenclature § Trade names](/wiki/Drug_nomenclature#Trade_names "Drug nomenclature"). For other products and services, see [Brand name](/wiki/Brand#Brand_name "Brand").

"D/b/a" redirects here. For other uses, see [DBA (disambiguation)](/wiki/DBA_(disambiguation) "DBA (disambiguation)").

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Intellectual_property "Template:Intellectual property") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Intellectual_property "Template talk:Intellectual property") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Intellectual_property "Special:EditPage/Template:Intellectual property") |

A **trade name**, also known as a **trading name**, **business name** or **operating name**, is a [pseudonym](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym") used by companies and other organizations that do not operate under their registered legal name.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-LoPucki_Page_60-2) Registering the trade name with a relevant government body is often required.

In a number of countries, the phrase "**trading as**" (abbreviated to **t/a**) is used to designate a trade name. In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the phrase "**doing business as**" (abbreviated to **DBA**, **dba**, **d.b.a.**, or **d/b/a**) is used,[[2]](#cite_note-LoPucki_Page_60-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Pinkerton-3) among others, such as **assumed business name**[[4]](#cite_note-4) or **fictitious business name**.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), "**operating as**" (abbreviated to **o/a**) and "*trading as*" are used, although "*doing business as*" is also sometimes used.[[6]](#cite_note-business-registration-6)

A company typically uses a trade name to conduct business using a simpler name rather than using their formal and often lengthier name. Trade names are also used when a preferred name cannot be registered, often because it may already be registered or is too similar to a name that is already registered.

Online platforms such as [Wix](/wiki/Wix.com "Wix.com"), Namelix, Looka, [LegalZoom](/wiki/LegalZoom "LegalZoom"), and [Elementor](/wiki/Elementor "Elementor") also provide tools for creating and searching for trade names as part of establishing a business website.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Legal aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Legal aspects")]

Using one or more fictitious business names does not create additional separate legal entities.[[3]](#cite_note-Pinkerton-3) The distinction between a registered legal name and a fictitious business name, or trade name, is important because fictitious business names do not always identify the entity that is [legally responsible](/wiki/Legal_liability "Legal liability").

Legal agreements (such as [contracts](/wiki/Contract "Contract")) are normally made using the registered legal name of the business. If a corporation fails to consistently adhere to such important legal formalities like using its registered legal name in contracts, it may be subject to [piercing of the corporate veil](/wiki/Piercing_the_corporate_veil "Piercing the corporate veil").[[9]](#cite_note-Plimpton-9)

In [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), trade names are generally treated as [proper nouns](/wiki/Proper_noun "Proper noun").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: By country")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Trade_name "Special:EditPage/Trade name") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(May 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Argentina")]

In [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), a trade name is known as a *nombre de fantasía* ('fantasy' or 'fiction' name), and the legal name of business is called a *razón social* (social name).

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Brazil")]

In [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), a trade name is known as a *nome fantasia* ('fantasy' or 'fiction' name), and the legal name of business is called *razão social* (social name).

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Canada")]

In some [Canadian](/wiki/Canada "Canada") [jurisdictions](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), such as [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), when a businessperson writes a trade name on a contract, invoice, or cheque, they must also add the legal name of the business.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[Numbered companies](/wiki/Numbered_company "Numbered company") will very often operate as something other than their legal name, which is unrecognizable to the public.

### Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Chile")]

In [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), a trade name is known as a *nombre de fantasía* ('fantasy' or 'fiction' name), and the legal name of business is called a *razón social* (social name).

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ireland")]

In [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), businesses are legally required to register business names where these differ from the surname(s) of the sole trader or partners, or the legal name of a company. The [Companies Registration Office](/wiki/Companies_Registration_Office_(Ireland) "Companies Registration Office (Ireland)") publishes a searchable register of such business names.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Japan")]

In [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), the word [yagō](/wiki/Yag%C5%8D "Yagō") (屋号) is used.

### Kenya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Kenya")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | **This section is empty.** You can help by [adding to it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=). *(June 2024)* |

### Nigeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Nigeria")]

In [Colonial Nigeria](/wiki/Colonial_Nigeria "Colonial Nigeria"), certain tribes had members that used a variety of trading names to conduct business with the Europeans. Two examples were King [Perekule VII](/wiki/George_Oruigbiji_Pepple "George Oruigbiji Pepple") of [Bonny](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bonny "Kingdom of Bonny"), who was known as *Captain Pepple* in trade matters, and King [Jubo Jubogha](/wiki/Jubo_Jubogha "Jubo Jubogha") of [Opobo](/wiki/Opobo "Opobo"), who bore the pseudonym *Captain Jaja*. Both Pepple and Jaja would bequeath their trade names to their royal descendants as official [surnames](/wiki/Surname "Surname") upon their deaths.

### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Singapore")]

In [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), there is no filing requirement for a "trading as" name, but there are requirements for disclosure of the underlying business or company's registered name and unique entity number.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), there is no filing requirement for a *business name*, defined as "any name under which someone carries on business" that, for a company or limited liability partnership, "is not its registered name", but there are requirements for disclosure of the owner's true name and some restrictions on the use of certain names and *sensitive words*, and there are also regulations concerning disclosure of the *company name* (the legal name of the company) for a company, the name of the owner for a sole trader, or the names of the partners for a partnership.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The [Office for Students](/wiki/Office_for_Students "Office for Students"), the higher education regulator for England, uses the term *trading name* in the register of higher education providers, and requires these to be registered.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The [Charity Commission of England and Wales](/wiki/Charity_Commission_of_England_and_Wales "Charity Commission of England and Wales") uses the terms *working name* and *operating name* on the register of charities,[[17]](#cite_note-17) with the term *working name* being used in the [Charities Act 2011](/wiki/Charities_Act_2011 "Charities Act 2011") (as amended by the Charities Act 2022).[[18]](#cite_note-18) The term *operating name* is also used for government agencies.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: United States")]

A minority of U.S. states, including [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), still use the term *trade name* to refer to "doing business as" (DBA) names.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In most U.S. states now, however, DBAs are officially referred to using other terms. Almost half of the states, including [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), use the terms *assumed business name* or *assumed name*;[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) nearly as many, including [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), use the term *fictitious name*.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

For [consumer protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection") purposes, many U.S. jurisdictions require businesses operating with fictitious names to file a DBA statement, though names including the first and last name of the owner may be accepted.[[24]](#cite_note-24) This also reduces the possibility of two local businesses operating under the same name, although some jurisdictions do not provide exclusivity for a name, or may allow more than one party to register the same name. Note, though, that this is not a substitute for filing a [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") application. A DBA filing carries no legal weight in establishing trademark rights.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In the U.S., trademark rights are acquired by use in commerce, but there can be substantial benefits to filing a trademark application.[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Sole proprietors](/wiki/Sole_proprietorship "Sole proprietorship") are the most common users of DBAs. Sole proprietors are individual business owners who run their businesses themselves. Since most people in these circumstances use a business name other than their own name,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] it is often necessary for them to get DBAs.

Generally, a DBA must be registered with a local or state government, or both, depending on the jurisdiction. For example, California, Texas and Virginia require a DBA to be registered with each county (or [independent city](/wiki/Independent_city_(United_States) "Independent city (United States)") in the case of Virginia) where the owner does business. Maryland and Colorado have DBAs registered with a state agency. Virginia also requires corporations and LLCs to file a copy of their registration with the county or city to be registered with the State Corporation Commission.

DBA statements are often used in conjunction with a [franchise](/wiki/Franchising "Franchising"). The franchisee will have a legal name under which it may sue and be sued, but will conduct business under the franchiser's [brand name](/wiki/Brand_name "Brand name") (which the public would recognize). A typical real-world example can be found in a well-known pricing [mistake](/wiki/Mistake_(contract_law) "Mistake (contract law)") case, *Donovan v. RRL Corp.* (2001),[[27]](#cite_note-27) where the named defendant, RRL Corporation, was a Lexus [car dealership](/wiki/Car_dealership "Car dealership") doing business as "[Lexus](/wiki/Lexus "Lexus") of [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster,_California "Westminster, California")", but remaining a separate legal entity from Lexus, a division of [Toyota Motor Sales, USA, Inc.](/wiki/Toyota_Motor_Sales,_USA "Toyota Motor Sales, USA")

In [California](/wiki/California "California"), filing a DBA statement also requires that a notice of the fictitious name be published as a [public legal notice](/wiki/Public_notice "Public notice") in local [newspapers](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper") for some set period to inform the public of the owner's intent to operate under an [assumed name](/wiki/Assumed_name "Assumed name"). The intention of the law is to protect the public from fraud, by compelling the business owner to first file or register his fictitious business name with the county clerk, and then making a further public record of it by publishing it in a newspaper.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Several other states, such as [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), require print notices as well.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Uruguay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_name&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Uruguay")]

In [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay"), a trade name is known as a *nombre fantasía*, and the legal name of business is called a *razón social*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
